Proper Rest Is Also a Way of Learning
I've been prepping a small project lately while also helping a bunch of friends who DM'd me. I noticed a lot of them share a bad habit — sharing some experience here

When learning new tech, you'll feel all kinds of pressure — career-switch pressure, life pressure, financial pressure. These pressures are part of the test when learning new things. But don't let them break your learning plan or force yourself to "learn it in X days or X months". That just plants a seed of unease.
If you have natural talent and learn quickly, fine.

But what if you don't hit your self-imposed deadline? Your brain starts spinning unconscious anxieties — "What if my career switch fails?", "What if I never get this?" Those negative thoughts pile on under the radar. You can ignore them at first, but over time you'll feel more powerless, and the original passion fades.
Like an hourglass — pressure keeps accumulating, until it overflows or breaks.
Sometimes the volume of new knowledge alone is too much to absorb, generating its own pressure.

Hold a simple realization: you're not a hero. You can't double up on schoolwork and saving the world like Spider-Man. Learn at the right intensity. Don't pile excessive pressure on yourself. Sometimes give yourself a break.
Proper rest is also a form of learning

We start learning new tech with a lot of passion and curiosity. After hitting some level, we start job hunting and usually hit walls — sometimes luck brings an offer, but the wall-hitting outcome dominates.
At that point some people grind harder; some get crushed. But is just grinding the right move? I can say

. I once stayed up till 2–3 AM trying to understand a for loop concept. Surprised? Yeah, the loop concept stumped me. I figured I'd just power through it, kept burning the midnight oil, no rest. Result: my body started crashing — mouth ulcers, skin issues, all kinds of stuff popped up.
I was pressured both by life and by being stuck. So I gave myself a few days off (about 1–2 weeks). No coding whatsoever. Pure relaxation, gaming, slacking.
When I came back to the stuck spot, I got it instantly. Sometimes our state of mind, body, or environment affects absorption. You can see why engineers solve problems on bathroom breaks (huh?).

After that experience, proper rest became part of my learning plan. On weekends I might game for 2 hours, watch TV for 2 hours, watch a movie over dinner. When I come back to writing logic or absorbing concepts, it's much easier.

This post is also a reminder for myself: don't pile too much pressure on yourself. Sometimes too much pressure is the very obstacle to learning. Some pressure grows you. Too much pressure regresses or kills you.
